questions.question_text: At which OSI layer does Azure Application Gateway operate?

a.explanation: Layer 3 handles IP routing. Application Gateway operates at a higher layer, using HTTP/HTTPS content for routing decisions.

b.explanation: Layer 4 (TCP/UDP) is where Azure Load Balancer operates. Application Gateway goes higher to leverage application-layer content.

c.explanation: Application Gateway is a Layer 7 load balancer. It can inspect HTTP/HTTPS request content — including the URL path and hostname — to make intelligent routing decisions.

d.explanation: Layer 2 is the switching/MAC address layer. Application Gateway operates far above this at the application layer.

questions.question_text: What is the role of the 'listener' in Azure Application Gateway?

a.text: It probes backend pool resources to check their health
a.explanation: Health checking is done by health probes, which are a separate component. The listener handles incoming traffic inspection.

b.text: It inspects incoming HTTP/HTTPS traffic and matches it to a routing rule
b.explanation: The listener receives incoming requests and reads the HTTP hostname, path, and protocol. It then matches the request to a configured rule that determines which backend pool receives the traffic.

c.text: It assigns SSL certificates to backend virtual machines
c.explanation: SSL certificate management (termination) is a feature of Application Gateway, but the listener's role is traffic inspection and rule matching, not certificate assignment to VMs.

d.text: It replaces the need for a public IP address on the Application Gateway
d.explanation: Application Gateway still requires a frontend IP configuration (public or private). The listener operates above the IP layer.

questions.question_text: A company wants to route requests to contoso.com/images to one backend pool and contoso.com/videos to a different backend pool. Which Application Gateway feature enables this?

a.explanation: Multi-site routing differentiates traffic based on the hostname (e.g., contoso.com vs fabricam.com), not the URL path. This scenario requires path-based routing.

b.explanation: Path-based routing allows Application Gateway to direct traffic to different backend pools based on the URL path. /images routes to one pool; /videos routes to another — enabling right-sized resource allocation.

c.explanation: Port-based rules are a Load Balancer concept operating at Layer 4. Routing by URL path requires Layer 7 Application Gateway with path-based routing.

d.explanation: Traffic Manager routes by DNS resolution to different endpoints, not by URL path. Path inspection is done by Application Gateway.

questions.question_text: A company hosts two separate internal portals — contoso.com and fabricam.com — and wants both to resolve to the same public IP address while serving completely different content. Which Application Gateway feature enables this?

a.explanation: Path-based routing differentiates by URL path on the same hostname. This scenario requires differentiation by hostname, which is multi-site routing.

b.explanation: Multi-site routing allows Application Gateway to inspect the HTTP Host header and route contoso.com traffic to one backend pool and fabricam.com traffic to a different pool — all on the same public IP.

c.explanation: Health probes check backend resource availability. They do not route traffic based on hostname. Multi-site routing is the correct feature.

d.text: Two separate Application Gateways — one per domain
d.explanation: A single Application Gateway can host multiple sites using multi-site routing. Deploying two gateways would be wasteful and unnecessary.

questions.question_text: Which component is present in Application Gateway but NOT in standard Azure Load Balancer?

a.explanation: Both Application Gateway and Load Balancer use backend pools. The backend pool is not a differentiating component.

b.explanation: Both services use health probes to check backend resource availability. This is not unique to Application Gateway.

c.explanation: The listener is unique to Application Gateway. It inspects HTTP/HTTPS content (host, path, protocol) to make Layer 7 routing decisions — a capability Load Balancer does not have.

d.explanation: Both Load Balancer and Application Gateway require a frontend IP configuration. This is not a differentiating component.

questions.question_text: What is the minimum number of routing rules required on an Application Gateway to function?

a.explanation: At least one rule is required to define what the Application Gateway does with incoming traffic. Without a rule, the gateway cannot route traffic.

b.explanation: At minimum, one rule must be configured. This rule links the listener (frontend conditions) to a backend pool and HTTP settings so traffic can flow.

c.text: Two — one for HTTP and one for HTTPS
c.explanation: You can operate with a single rule for just one protocol. A second rule is optional and added only if you need to handle a second protocol or path/host.

d.text: Five — one per backend pool member
d.explanation: Rules map listeners to backend pools, not individual VMs. One rule can serve an entire backend pool.

questions.question_text: A video streaming company wants to route users globally to the nearest edge location for faster content delivery, using Microsoft's backbone network. Which Azure load balancing solution is most appropriate?

a.explanation: Azure Load Balancer is a regional, Layer 4 service. It does not use a CDN or Microsoft's backbone network for global content delivery.

b.explanation: Application Gateway is regional, not global. It does not provide CDN capabilities or edge-based content delivery.

c.explanation: Front Door is a global CDN and Layer 7 load balancing service that uses Microsoft's backbone network to bring content closer to users worldwide — ideal for media streaming.

d.explanation: Traffic Manager provides global DNS-based routing, but it does not accelerate content delivery via CDN. Front Door is the appropriate choice for content delivery scenarios.

questions.question_text: What protocols does Azure Application Gateway handle? (Select all that apply)

a.explanation: Application Gateway is designed for HTTP/HTTPS traffic. HTTP is a core supported protocol.

b.explanation: HTTPS is fully supported. Application Gateway can also perform SSL termination for HTTPS traffic.

c.explanation: Raw TCP traffic (non-HTTP) is handled by Azure Load Balancer, not Application Gateway. App Gateway requires HTTP/HTTPS for its Layer 7 features.

d.explanation: UDP is handled by Azure Load Balancer at Layer 4. Application Gateway only processes HTTP and HTTPS.

questions.question_text: An image-editing service and a video-editing service need very different compute resources. Both are accessed via the same Application Gateway. Which architecture best supports this using path-based routing?

a.text: One shared backend pool with all VMs mixed together
a.explanation: A mixed pool defeats the purpose of path-based routing. You want separate pools so the right resources serve the right workload.

b.text: Two separate backend pools — one for image editing, one for video editing — with path rules directing traffic to each
b.explanation: Path-based routing sends /images traffic to a pool of lighter VMs for image editing, and /videos traffic to a pool of high-compute VMs for video editing. This optimises both cost and performance.

c.text: Two separate Application Gateways — one for each service
c.explanation: Multiple backend pools on a single Application Gateway handle this scenario efficiently. Two separate gateways would be more expensive and harder to manage.

d.text: A Traffic Manager profile pointing to two separate Load Balancers
d.explanation: Traffic Manager routes by DNS and does not understand URL paths. It cannot route /images vs /videos to different resources.

questions.question_text: Is Azure Application Gateway a regional or global service?

a.text: Global — it can balance traffic across multiple Azure regions
a.explanation: Application Gateway is a regional service. For global Layer 7 load balancing, Azure Front Door is the appropriate service.

b.text: Regional — it operates within a single Azure region
b.explanation: Application Gateway is regional in scope. It manages HTTP/HTTPS traffic within one region. Front Door covers global scenarios.

c.text: It depends on the SKU — Standard is regional, WAF is global
c.explanation: Both Standard and WAF SKUs of Application Gateway are regional. The global vs regional distinction is not tied to the WAF SKU.

d.text: It can be either — you choose during creation like Load Balancer
d.explanation: Unlike Azure Load Balancer (which does have a limited global tier option), Application Gateway is strictly regional.
